skill.name: gh-fix-ci
skill.description: Inspect GitHub pull request checks with the GitHub CLI, collect failing GitHub Actions log context, and guide an approved fix workflow.
skill.summary: Debug failing GitHub Actions checks on pull requests and prepare an approval-based fix plan.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command patterns, but most are Markdown examples for GitHub CLI and Python commands rather than executable code. The confirmed risk is legitimate but elevated: the skill asks the agent to use authenticated gh commands, network access, GitHub Actions logs, and approved code changes. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious behavior was found.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated GitHub CLI Commands With Elevated Access
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: the skill instructs agents to run gh authentication checks and GitHub Actions inspection commands with network access and possibly escalated permissions. This is expected for CI debugging, but it can expose repository metadata, check results, and log content to the agent workflow.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly instruct use of gh auth, PR checks, run views, logs, and API log downloads. The behavior is legitimate for this skill, but the privileged networked context is clear.

medium_findings.title: Approval-Based Code Modification Workflow
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: the skill is designed to implement fixes after user approval. This is not malicious, but it gives the agent filesystem impact and should be used only in trusted repositories with reviewed plans.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The workflow explicitly moves from failure summary to plan creation and implementation after approval. The approval gate lowers risk, but file modification capability remains part of the skill intent.

low_findings.title: Ruby Backtick Execution Detections Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: the static analyzer treated inline backticks in Markdown as Ruby or shell backtick execution. The file contains usage examples and command names, not Ruby code or executable backtick syntax.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: SKILL.md is Markdown guidance, and the backtick tokens delimit commands or parameter names. No Ruby file or Ruby execution context exists in the scanned file.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: lines flagged as weak cryptography are description and workflow text. No hashing, encryption, MD5, SHA-1, or cryptographic implementation appears at these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain skill metadata and a PR input instruction. I found no cryptographic operation or weak algorithm usage in those lines.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL And Reconnaissance Detections Are Benign
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: the hardcoded URL is an example GitHub pull request URL, and the detailsUrl instruction is used to identify a GitHub Actions run. No external exfiltration endpoint or system reconnaissance command is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations reference GitHub Actions metadata and an example GitHub URL. The context is CI log retrieval, not host enumeration or data exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Escalated gh Authentication Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill advises rerunning gh auth status with sandbox_permissions=require_escalated when sandboxing blocks access. This can be necessary for GitHub authentication, but it should be approved only for trusted repositories and accounts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The escalation guidance is explicit and tied to GitHub authentication. The risk is contextual rather than malicious.

dangerous_patterns.title: Networked GitHub Actions Log Retrieval
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow fetches PR check data and logs through gh commands and gh api. Logs can contain sensitive build details, so users should review repository trust and token scope before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands directly retrieve check metadata, run data, and logs from GitHub. This is core functionality, but it carries information exposure risk.

content.value_statement: Failing pull request checks can hide the useful error inside long GitHub Actions logs. This skill guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code through check inspection, failure summaries, and approved fixes.
actual_capabilities: Checks GitHub pull request status with the gh CLI.
Retrieves GitHub Actions run details and failure logs.
Summarizes failing check names, run links, and log snippets.
Separates GitHub Actions failures from external check providers.
Creates a fix plan and waits for user approval before edits.
limitations: Requires an authenticated gh CLI with suitable repository access.
Does not debug Buildkite or other external check providers.
The referenced helper script is not included in this package.
Cannot guarantee logs are available for running or expired jobs.

use_cases.title: Triage a blocked pull request
use_cases.description: Find the failing GitHub Actions check and extract the most relevant log lines before changing code.

use_cases.title: Review CI failures across a team repository
use_cases.description: Use a consistent workflow to gather check status, summarize failures, and prepare a fix plan.

use_cases.title: Validate a CI repair workflow
use_cases.description: Confirm that a proposed fix maps back to the failing check and suggest follow-up verification.

prompt_templates.title: Check my current PR
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for the active branch.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use gh-fix-ci to inspect the current branch PR, summarize failing GitHub Actions checks, and wait for my approval before making changes.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect a specific PR
prompt_templates.scenario: User has a PR number or URL.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use gh-fix-ci on PR <number-or-url>. Show the failing check name, relevant log snippet, and a short fix plan before editing files.

prompt_templates.title: Separate external checks
prompt_templates.prompt: Use gh-fix-ci to review the PR checks. Fetch GitHub Actions logs only, and report external checks with their details URL as out of scope.

prompt_templates.title: Repair and verify CI
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow with implementation after approval.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use gh-fix-ci to inspect the failing GitHub Actions logs, propose a minimal fix plan, implement it after I approve, and recommend the exact verification command.

output_examples.input: Inspect the CI failure on my current PR.
output_examples.output: The response identifies the failing check, links the GitHub Actions run, summarizes the relevant error, and asks for approval before editing.

output_examples.input: Fix the failing workflow on PR 123.
output_examples.output: The response reports the check status, explains the likely cause, proposes a focused fix plan, and waits for confirmation.

output_examples.input: This PR has GitHub Actions and Buildkite checks.
output_examples.output: The response analyzes GitHub Actions logs and marks external provider details as out of scope with the provider URL.

best_practices: Use a GitHub token with the smallest scopes needed for the repository.
Review the proposed fix plan before allowing file changes.
Run the relevant local tests before asking GitHub Actions to recheck.
anti_patterns: Do not run this skill against untrusted repositories with privileged credentials.
Do not paste private CI logs into unrelated public issues or chats.
Do not treat external provider checks as GitHub Actions failures.

faq.question: Does this skill require GitHub authentication?
faq.answer: Yes. It depends on the gh CLI and repository access to read PR checks and workflow logs.

faq.question: Can it fix Buildkite failures?
faq.answer: No. It reports external check URLs but focuses on GitHub Actions failures only.

faq.question: Will it change files automatically?
faq.answer: The workflow says to create a plan first and implement only after explicit approval.

faq.question: Can it inspect the current branch PR?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow prefers the current branch PR when the user does not provide a PR number or URL.

faq.question: What information can it expose?
faq.answer: It can surface repository check metadata and CI log snippets available to the authenticated gh account.

faq.question: Is the bundled helper script available?
faq.answer: No evidence found in this package. Only SKILL.md is present in the scanned file structure.
